We can start acceptance testing as soon as any of the parts of the project is complete.

It demonstrates system conformance to
	- customer requiments
	- operationel processes
	- maintainance processes

Usually based on the requirement specifications
Independent from other types of testing
mostly executed by the end users (and/or PO)

Acceptance tests ensures that the system satisfies the customer's needs.
	- tests against the requirements specification
Should be performed with the customer's inv

cation
Should be performed with the customer's involvement
	operational users
	beta customers
	comparny executives

Commen in contractual situations
	successful tests completion leds the customer to accept the system
	Unsuccesfull test completion cen entitle the customer to reject the product(based on the written contract)

SLA ( service level Agrement)

Validation
	at the stage the product is not expected to fail
	the objective is not to find defects but to ensure that everything works accordin

fects but to ensure that everything works according to specifications
	successfull acceptance tests can give the green light to product deployment.

Acceptance tests
	clarify requirements
		comment ground between customers and developers
		in Agile, based on user stories they can procide a measure of "doneness"
	Must be comprehensive
		the whole system is tested
		In agile and iterative SDLC's(software development life cicle)	it involces testeing all functionalities for each iteration
	do not ch

all functionalities for each iteration
	do not change even if product implementation does.

Types of Acceptance tests:

• User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
• Operational Acceptance Testing
• Validation of the products’ operations in a real situation
• Contract and Regulations Testing
• Legal bonds, safety standards (banking, pharmaceutical),
governmental regulations (defense)
• Alpha Testing. In-house
• Beta Testing. At the customer’s premises
• Pilot phase. Product release
on a limited geographica

ot phase. Product release
on a limited geographical or logical scale

UAT - User acceptance tests
	a test from the perspective of the users and other stateholders
	not just a tests of the software, nor of the functionality, performance, reliability and security nor of the usability of the system
	the nearest we get to "the real thing" without actually taking the risk of releasing the system into service.

part of integration testing

its importen because between 60 and 80% of all internet traffic is API calls
subcutaneous testing (under the skin testing, explain further)


potential API errors:
	version changes
	endpoint name or paramenters changes.
	permissions changes(user that can only read, should not be able to write)
	server issues
	Network issues
	
elements to test:
	Data format validation, to prevent fraudulent client requests.
	Authentocation, usually with a token
	permissions, since the

tion, usually with a token
	permissions, since there might be more than one authorisation level
	Proper use of HTTP status codes besides 200 OK and 404 Not Found!
		201 created
		400 bad request (malformed request)
		401 Unauthorized (well formed request, but the client does ot have permission)
		500 internal server error (the api has a bug)
		204 correct deletion

further tests:
	how do a GET request behave when they return no data
	POST, PUT and PATCH requests:
		extremely long text values
		U

d PATCH requests:
		extremely long text values
		Unsanitised strings
	whether put requests should require all values to be filled
	how do delete requests behave

Setting up and tearing down unit tests:

Setup and tear down

set up - the "contructor of the testing class - fill out more here

tear down - closes all open connections - fill out more here




Test doubles: generic term for any case where you replace replace a production object for testing purposes.

	its fake objects that subsitute other objects in the codebase
	They allow yo: Test a unit in total isolation
			Substitute components not yet developed
			Speed up test execution

Types of test d

eloped
			Speed up test execution

Types of test doubles:

	mock: sending something to a database
	stub: Emulating incoming call. (data coming from an end point or database)


Unit Testing: best practices:

	Each unit test must verify one and only one behaviour.
		avoid chaining arrange, act and assert sections
		unit tests may easyly become an integration test
	Avoid branching (no if,switch,while or for)

Cover the business logic, and not much else. (only write unit tests if there is business l

lse. (only write unit tests if there is business logic)

	Code is not an assert but a liability
		more code -> higher risk of bugs
	if code architecture changes, tests may also change.

Act section must only be one line of code
	otherwise there may be errors in code design
	if the test fails with a big act, its hard to know what went wrong.

use a comprehensive approach to test case design
	perform black-box test desing
		look for numering boundaries and thier surroundings
	Do positive and negat

ries and thier surroundings
	Do positive and negative testing
	complete the suite with tests chases

All unit tests should be run upon every code change, no matter how small -> regression testing
Good code design and structure is of the essense
	tight couplign prevent writing unit tests
write pure methods as often as posible (return the same output based on the same input)

Unit Testing anti-patterns
	never test private methods directly
		testing is then based in observable behaviour
		tests bec

is then based in observable behaviour
		tests become coupled ti implementation
			tests are no longer resistant to refactoring
		if you need to tests a private method, use the white box technique and your knowledge of the code to give the tests the parameters its needs to reach said private method.


Do never expose private state

try to write a tests for the what the user would see.

Do not leak domain knowlodfe to the unit test
	Always use hardcoded values or mock!

avoid code polution! - cha

dcoded values or mock!

avoid code polution! - changing your code for tests


Private vs Shared dependensices

	private dependencies occur between code units.
	shared dependencies are those that 
		are shared between tests
		tend to slow down the execution
		provide means for tests to effect each others outcome
			fx databases
				if one deletes a row and anthoer tests searches for it, the execution of the first test makes the second test fail.
	Unit tests involving shared dependencies might be

Unit tests involving shared dependencies might be considered integration tests.

for the exam, be able to say what a unit test and integration test is for you.

Volatile dependencies

	Introduce a requirement to configure a runtime environment
		a database an API service
	contain nondeterministic behaviour
		a random numbergenerator
very difficult to test
a database is both a shared and a volitile dependency

The classical Approach: a single unit of behaviour

(Detroit approach)

use tests doubl

of behaviour

(Detroit approach)

use tests doubles only to replace shared dependencies.
"sociable" unit tests: collaborators are not mocked
isolation of the unit tests, not tof the code under test

The London approach ( the mockist approach) a single unit of code

recommends to replace all dependencies by test doubles except for immutable dependencies(constants , enums)

solidary unit tests: collaborators are mocked.
total isolation of the code under test.
benefits: small, if a error is found

under test.
benefits: small, if a error is found you know precisly where the error is.

code is written once, but read alot of times - write code to be read





Type: 



Black box - specification - based -  (sometimes low experience with code base) often written based on the specifications



black - box techniques:

1\. equivalence Partitioning

2\. Boundary value analysis

3\. decision table testing

4\. State transition testing





**1 Equivalence Partitioning:**



 	Input Partions:



 	similar inputs can be grouped together (positive integers, alphabetic characte

d together (positive integers, alphabetic characters)

 	any partitions calue can used for testing (often a middle value)

 	numbers: zero is a special case that consitute a partition of its own

 	input partions (based on input values)

 	

example: program that accepts integers between -10000 and 10000

 	should be split in valid and non valid partitions:

 	valid:

 	integer between -10000 and -1 (inclusive)

 	for 0

 	integer between 1 and 10000

for 0

 	integer between 1 and 10000 (inclusive)

 	non-valid:

 	below -10000

 	above 10000

 	non-integer numbers (edge chase)

 	non-numeric characters(edge chase)



 	this would translate into 3 "positive" tests(partitions) and 4 "negative" tests (partitions)



Always remember edge chases!



 	Output Partitions:



 	These are based on outputs!

 	Depending on the input, we expect different Outputs. Kinda the same as input partion

different Outputs. Kinda the same as input partions, find the inputs that give the expected outputs.

 	

 	



**In class exercise:**



Bank account

 	Suppose you have a bank account that offers variable interest rates:



 	0.5 per cent for the first 10000 kr credit

 	1 per cent for the next 10000 kr

 	1.5 per cent for the rest



 	If you wanted to check that the bank was handling your account correctly:



 	What input partitions might you use?

&n

What input partitions might you use?

 	

 	Valid:

 	1. 0 > 10000.000 2. 10000.000 > 20000.000 3.  200000.000 < MAX

 	none-Valid:

 	1. 0 2. MIN < 0 if it can recieve inputs: 3. non-integer numbers 4. non-numeric characters

 	

 	

 	What test case values could be inferred from said partitions?



**2. Boundary Value Analysis**



 	Partitions have boundaries

 	errors tend to cluster around boundaries

 	mostly bases on greater

around boundaries

 	mostly bases on greater or lower than and loops

 	works well in combination with equivalence partitioning

 	

 	Use the 3 boundary values approach - find the bounderies and test around them. ex 1 -99 test around 0 , 1 , 2 and 98,99,100

 	

 	Error testing: try to have the application raise exceptions and errors - if you are using your own errors you need to write tests to accomidate those.

 	Parameter testing: Try edge chases in method

nbsp;	Parameter testing: Try edge chases in method/function parameters.



**In class exercise:**



 	

 	A wholesaler sells printer cartridges. The minimum order quantity is 5. There is a 20% discount for orders of 100 or more printer cartridges. You 	have been asked to prepare test cases using various values for the number of printer cartridges ordered.



 	Use black-box analysis to identify a comprehensive series of test cases:



 	Identify the corresponding equivalence

nd write the corresponding unit tests in the language and unit test framework of your choice



**In class Exercise:**



 	A password field accepts a minimum of 6 characters and a maximum of 10 characters. Define:



 	Its corresponding equivalence partitions and test case values:

 	valid: 1. 5 >= 10 8          5,6,7  9,10,11   

 	non-valid: 

 	2. 1 >= 5         2          0,1,2   4,5,6            

'	3. 10 <           542        10,11,12			

 	4. 0 		  0	     0

542        10,11,12			

 	4. 0 		  0	     0

 	

 	The boundary values and resulting test case values with a 3-boundary value approach

 	The final list of test case values

 	

 	0,1,2,4,5,6,7,8,9,10,11,12,542



**In Class Exercise:**



	You are testing the payment functionality of an e-shop. The system receives a positive amount of purchases in kroner with an accuracy of 1 øre. 	Based on this value, a discount is calculated according to the following rules:

is calculated according to the following rules:



 	Amount	Discount

 	Up to 300 kr	0%

 	Over 300 kr, up to 800 kr	5%

 	Over 800 kr	10%

 	Use black-box analysis to identify a comprehensive series of test cases:



 	Identify the corresponding equivalence partitions and propose test cases based on them

 	Use 3-value boundary value analysis to identify further test cases



 	valid: 

 	1. 1 <=300        50   0%       0,1,2        299,300,301

&nbs

50   0%       0,1,2        299,300,301

 	2. 300 <= 800     500  5%       300,301,302  799,800,801

 	3. 800 <          5000 10%      800,801,802

 	non-valid:

 	4. 0 	          0    error    0

 	5. < 0           -50   error    -2,-1,0

 	

 	Write down the full resulting list of test cases:



 	-50,-2,-1,0,1,2,50,299,300,301,302,500,799,800,801,802,5000

 		

 	Implement the discount calculation function in code and write the correspond

culation function in code and write the corresponding unit tests in the language and unit test framework of your choice

**Hard you can kick, software you can only curse at.**



***Introduction:***



Why do we test - because software fails.





**Why does software fail?:**



Developers makes mistakes



Errors in sprecifications, design, code: bad user Stories.



presure: deadline, complexity of systems, change of technologies



**What does testing do?**



helps examining the code

finding errors

preventing defects / preventing errors from happening



**When is a test good?**



When it finds errors! / br

en is a test good?**



When it finds errors! / breaks the system

Dont write millions of test, but good tests.



**How much should we test?**



Depends on how much time and money we have



***Terminology:***



**Debugging vs Testing:**



**Debugging:**

The developers indentify the cause of bugs or defects in code and correct them.

Correct / fix code.

**Testing:**

Find bugs / you dont always fix the bugs.



**Static testing.** the code is not executed (linting / sonarcube)

**Dynamic t

is not executed (linting / sonarcube)

**Dynamic testing.** Execution of code with test data.



**Positive vs Negative testing:**



**Happy path vs unhappy path.**



**Positive (happy).** The code is supposed to execute correctly.

**Negative tested (unhappy).** The code is supposed to fail / try to break the code.



***Testing Concepts:***





**Regression testing:**

Does the new code, effect the rest of the system? if it does, a regression has been found.



Test basis

The body of knowl

n has been found.



Test basis

The body of knowledge used as the basis for test analysis and design

•Quality Management= Quality Assurance + Quality Control

•Quality Assurance (QA). Proactive.Making sure that processes are undertaken correctly

•Quality Control (QC). Reactive.Making sure that the desired level of quality is achieved

•Testing. Enforcement of quality control by finding defects in a product





**Verification vs Validation:**



Work product - Intermediate of deliverable of t

Work product - Intermediate of deliverable of the final product



**Verification:**

writing tests to verify the acceptance criteries are covered '

**Validation:**

The PO approving the product based the acceptance criteries based on the tests (verification).



**General Testing Principles (we will be asked about 4 of them at the exam)**



1. **When we test we can prove bugs exist, but we can never prove they dont exist.**



**2. The main purpose of testing is to find as many defects as

purpose of testing is to find as many defects as possible.**



**3. Defect clustering:**

 	-Defects are not distributed uniformly either in the software or 	 over time.

 	-The Pareto 80-20 rule: 20% of the code can have 80% of the errors.

**4. Tests ear out ( = pesticide paradox)**

 	- tests lose their ability to detect errors over time.

 	- Tests must be regularly reviewed and modified.

 	- with developers experience increasing, reduced testing might be fine.

**5. Testing is context de

testing might be fine.

**5. Testing is context dependent**

 	-Risk is a key aspect

 	-You have to think about what you test and how.

**6. Absence of errors fallacy**

 	- Never assume that a product is error free

 	- Even verified systems might not pass validation

**7. Exhaustive testing is impossible**

**8. Early testing is of the essence:** Reasons:

 	-Many errors have to do with specifications

 	-It is cheaper



Exhaustive Testing is Impossible!

The 4 T's : Traits - Task - Tone - Targets 



 	**Trait:** How you want the LLM to think when its answering questions,

 	You could give it roles or specify a specifig behavior

 	

 	**Task:** Be as specific as possible



 	**Tone:** Style of language that the LLM should use

 		- clear language

 		- straigtforward language

 		- stuff to avoid (fx hype language)

 		- use a formal or proffesionel language

 	**Target:**  







All these T'

language

All these T's are giving the LLM a context for who the answer is for and how it should construct said answer.

# Introduction to Large Language Models for Developers

Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming how developers build applications. This document introduces key concepts, architectures, and practical considerations for integrating LLMs into software systems.

## What Are Large Language Models?

LLMs are deep learning models trained on vast amounts of text data to understand and generate human-like language. They use transformer architectures with attention mechanisms to capture long-range d

with attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies in text.

Key characteristics include:
- Billions of parameters enabling rich language understanding
- Pre-trained on diverse internet-scale datasets
- Fine-tunable for specific tasks with smaller datasets
- Capable of few-shot and zero-shot learning

## Transformer Architecture Overview

The transformer model introduced by Vaswani et al. in 2017 relies entirely on attention mechanisms rather than recurrence or convolution. It consist

rather than recurrence or convolution. It consists of:

- **Encoder-decoder structure**: Encoders process input sequences; decoders generate outputs
- **Multi-head self-attention**: Allows the model to focus on different parts of the sequence simultaneously
- **Positional encoding**: Injects information about token positions since transformers don't inherently understand order
- **Feed-forward neural networks**: Applied independently to each position

Modern LLMs often use decoder-only transfor

ition

Modern LLMs often use decoder-only transformers like GPT series for autoregressive generation.

## Tokenization Strategies

Tokenization converts raw text into numerical inputs for models. Common strategies include:

- **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**: Iteratively merges frequent character pairs; balances vocabulary size and generalization
- **WordPiece**: Similar to BPE but considers likelihood of subword combinations
- **SentencePiece**: Unsupervised segmentation that works well with multil

upervised segmentation that works well with multilingual texts

Choosing a tokenizer impacts performance, especially for domain-specific terminology and rare words.

## Prompt Engineering Fundamentals

Prompt engineering shapes LLM behavior without retraining. Effective techniques include:

- **Explicit instructions**: Clearly define what you want from the model
- **Few-shot examples**: Provide demonstrations of desired output format
- **Chain-of-thought prompting**: Encourage step-by-step reaso

-thought prompting**: Encourage step-by-step reasoning for complex problems
- **Role playing**: Assign personas to influence tone and perspective

Well-crafted prompts improve accuracy, reduce hallucinations, and align responses with user intent.

## Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

RAG enhances LLM responses by incorporating external knowledge sources dynamically. The process involves:

1. Retrieving relevant documents based on query similarity
2. Augmenting the prompt with retrieved conte

rity
2. Augmenting the prompt with retrieved context
3. Generating an informed response using both internal and external knowledge

This approach improves factual grounding and reduces reliance on training data alone.

## Deployment Considerations

When deploying LLM-powered applications, consider:

- **Latency vs. cost trade-offs**: Larger models offer better quality but higher computational expense
- **Model quantization**: Techniques like 4-bit or 8-bit precision reduce memory footprint
- **C

t or 8-bit precision reduce memory footprint
- **Caching strategies**: Store results for repeated queries to optimize resource usage
- **Safety guardrails**: Filter harmful content and enforce ethical guidelines programmatically

Balancing these factors ensures scalable and responsible AI integration.

## Evaluation Metrics for LLM Applications

Measuring LLM effectiveness requires both automated metrics and human judgment:

- **BLEU/ROUGE scores**: Assess overlap between generated and reference

**: Assess overlap between generated and reference texts
- **Perplexity**: Measures how well a model predicts sample text (lower is better)
- **Human evaluation**: Rate coherence, relevance, and factual correctness directly

Combining quantitative measures with qualitative feedback yields holistic insights into system performance.

---
*These notes provide foundational knowledge for building and evaluating LLM-integrated systems.*

Static techniques test software without executing it
	They may find errors early in the life of the project
	They do not find failures, but causes of failures
	Static techniques:
	Review. Checking requirements and specification documents for errors
	before they are used in development.
	Usually manual
	Static Code Analysis. Analyse code for structural defects or 	systematic

programming weaknesses.
	• Usually automatic
	• Performed by linters and most IDEs

Reviews:

systematic examination of a

most IDEs

Reviews:

systematic examination of a document to find and remove errors.

	Objectives:
		• Finding defects
		• Gaining understanding
		• Generating discussion
		• Decision making by consensus
	Objects:
		• Requirement specifications
		• System designs
		• Code
		• Test plans
		• Test cases

Reviews and the V-model:

Every step can be reviewed.

A review is one of the earlies forms testing possible in a software development project (maybe beaten by risk analyst)

Two major types:
	Co

aybe beaten by risk analyst)

Two major types:
	Code review: can be performed one to one
	Document review Basic process:
		• The document is studied by the reviewers
		• The author is informed of the issues found
		• The author updates the document accordingly
		• A next iteration is performed, if necessary

Informal review
	• Documentation not required
	• Sometimes just a quick non-technical check
	• Inexpensive, but limited benefit
Walkthrough
	• Review meeting led by the author of the documen

• Review meeting led by the author of the document
	• It helps team members understand the document
	• Open-ended
	• Preparation and production of a report are optional

Technical review (including code review)
	• Documented, well-defined detection process
	• Peer review, desirably with no presence of management
	• Preparation and report
Inspection (most formal)
	• Led by a trained moderator
	• Peer examination of a document
	• Pre-meeting preparation
	• Production of an inspection report
	• Fo

ration
	• Production of an inspection report
	• Formal follow-up



Formal review process:

• Planning
	• Defining the purpose, scope, and time estimation of the review
	• Selecting staff and allocating roles
	• Defining the entry and exit criteria
	• Checking that the entry criteria is met
• Initiation
	• Distributing the work product(s) to review
	• Explaining to the participants the objectives, scope, work product and roles of the review




• Individual review
	• Reviewing the work product

Individual review
	• Reviewing the work product
	• Logging potential defects, questions and comments
	• Central phase (where most errors are caught)
	• Yet optional in low-formality reviews
• Communication and analysis
	• Communicating potential defects in a meeting, log, emails, etc.
	• Analysing the defects
	• Evaluating the review results against the exit criteria
	• The work product can be fixed, or completely rejected

Review roles and responsibilities:


Manager
	• Decides to review and w

onsibilities:


Manager
	• Decides to review and what to review
	• Designates staff and sets budget and timeframe
	• Not a part of the remaining review process
Author
	• Author of the work product to review
Moderator = review leader
	• Plans the review, follows up, mediates
Reviewers
Scribe = recorder
	• Documents issues


summary review report:

• Written by the moderator
• Contains
	• Date of the meeting/communication period
	• Names and roles of the participants
	• Duration

of the participants
	• Duration

		• E.g., pages of an SRS,
lines of code of a sw product

		• Usually in tabular form, sorted and colour coded

Success factors for reviews:


• Objectives must be clearly predefined and agreed
• Emphasis on learning and process improvement
	• Defects are welcomed
	• Defects must be expressed objectively
	• It is the documents, not the participants, that are reviewed
• Techniques must also be revi

, that are reviewed
• Techniques must also be reviewed
• Checklists or similar must be used

• Possible metrics to collect:
	• Time taken to review/inspect
	• Number of defects found
	• Percentage of project budget used/saved

typicaly helps finding: variables not being used, reference to variable with undefinded value, dead code, use of deprecated functionalities, violations of standeards, protential security issues.
